I think all of you have gone through some part of the onboarding, at least, even for those of you that are weak too, unless you joined this week itself, you've kind of have probably a lot of context, so I'll skip a little bit of that, but the whole goal of what we're doing is using services as the forcing function for us to figure out what great work looks like, right?
And so, part of building our platform is building for those of you that are on the delivery side, right?

And, but as we've kind of gone through this journey and grew really, really fast, especially at the beginning of this year, started to realize there's a lot of variance in our engagement and just expectations, right?
And part of that has also, and a lot of things we're kind of tackling right now, has caused churn, has caused a lot of inconsistencies in how we serve our customers, right?
Okay, so part of what we decided to do, and we talked amongst the leadership team,
So is change our approach to how we do SOWs and how we do engagements overall and what we're calling this a strategy sprint, right?

And so before I kind of dig into that, we can maybe go into the next slide and just the why behind it as far as what's not working today and why we're making this change that we think is going to be in a positive direction.
So the first thing is, no matter how much time you spend in sales, there's never perfect alignment and calibration, right?
Because you're not actually doing the work yet and you're kind of in this phase where you're almost dating still, right?

Like, so you're trying to put your best foot forward, the company is not giving you their, you know, behind the scenes and you're making a lot of assumptions and there's a lot of excitement and you just want to kick things off, right?
But then as we get post-sales, even if we do a really good job with disqualifying people and the sales process, there's always kind of this misalignment on...

And previously, our SLW was also really, really anchored on word count, or number of pages, and what we started to notice was people started to really try to focus on that, as if that was the be-all, end-all, and that's just a bad way to think about it.

The intent behind having some anchor in content or word count is mostly just, like, hey, we should be publishing regularly, you should be planting new seeds, you should be, you know, constantly doing work and getting output and learning from it.
And I apologize if hear my daughter in the background, she's super excited about this change.
And then number three is misalignment on content and the definition of done, right?
So, if you are, let's say, abnormal security versus ramp or your interwell, these customers all have a different...
That's definition of what done look like.
We've been in this engagement, for instance, with Vappy, and the CO was like, this content is good, but it's almost too good.
Can you all just lower the quality and go faster and publish more content?
We just want to learn as quickly as possible, right?
And so that's a very different approach than if you need legal review, if people want to line edit every single aspect of the content itself, right?
And so that requires a different alignment on expectations, right?
You're not going to commit to the same volume.
And so number four is no opportunity for GrowthX to opt out.

And so what's happening right now, we're kind of setting this precedent that like, hey, we're here to serve you no matter what, even if you're not a great customer to work with, and we want to change that.
We want to make this strategy sprint an opportunity for mutual alignment.
And if there is mutual alignment, we both have to agree to continue.
And this will give us an opportunity to just get bad customers out of the way and customers that have unrealistic expectations.

A quick note here is if you haven't gone through and actually built a publication yourself or ran a publication or been behind the scenes, you have probably no empathy for how much work it takes to do it well.
The word do it well.
And so you need strategy, you need managing editors, need writers, and you need people to help with the production, staging the content, hitting publish, constantly optimizing.
You need designers, you need a bunch of things in there.
These are all specialists.
And so if you're trying to do this correctly, you're going to have to hire a lot of specialists and then assemble that team together.
And so we're trying to, in this new SOW language, also communicate that better of, hey, this might not work out.
But even if it doesn't work out, we're going to give you an entire blueprint.
We're going to give you an AI-ready kind of
So, and everything you need to be successful on your own.
And the intent is for customers that want to try to do it on their own, they can go off and do it.
And most likely what they're going to find out is that this is a lot of work.
Unless they're willing to spend a huge amount of their own time on it, it's going to be probably more costly to them, right?
But that's okay with our platform.
And as we're developing it from the strategy sprint, we can convert people into platform-only customers as well.
And then we started to push too much on the programmatic SEO.
And prior to our platform being ready, we just weren't really ready to serve that in the best way possible.
And there was a lot of enablement internally as well we needed to do.
And it requires a lot of engineering involvement.
And so we can't just like take on these engagements and then ad hoc, you know, pull engineers into these engagements, right?
There needs to be some capacity planning.
And then the last piece, and then I think this is really like...
So, what we did, which is a mistake on my end, was we started putting new customers and aligning those with new directors that had new managing editors with new content managers.
So, you essentially had four layers of brand new with very little context.
And so, obviously, that was not a recipe for success.
So, we want to change that.
And so, the way to control that is to say the strategy sprint is where we're going to really set the strategy.
We're assembling a team that's going to do this.
I'll be leading that team, and we'll talk about who else is going to be involved in that.

And then the idea is by week six in this eight-week strategy sprint, we'll start to, once the client is committed to continuing, then we'll bring in directors and managing editors to hand it off for a production to kick off, right?
So, then the idea is in your pods, as you're receiving, if you will,

A new account, it will come with a strategy, with alignment, with definition of done, with an environment fully set up, and so it will be a lot more straightforward to know how to execute and how to hit the right, how to exceed expectations.

Whereas like right now, if you're a brand new director, a brand new managing editor, you're coming into this engagement, you're doing the kickoff, you're doing the onboarding, maybe it's your first time doing an onboarding, or maybe the last onboarding you did was six months ago, right?
Like, there's no way you're going to be amazing at onboarding, you just haven't had enough shots on goal, right?
And so we need to specialize this.
But also part of the strategy sprint, it requires a little bit of almost like sales skills, right?
Where you need to be able to sell that strategy, you need to be able to ask the right questions, get the right inputs.

And so, but then on our end, then we can focus, make sure the pods are really hitting it out of the park when it comes to executing against that strategy, holding the bar of quality and executing against it.
And also all the customer engagement things that need to happen, right?
Whether it's updating things, whether it's keeping, you know, pulling reports, being proactive with customers, going above and beyond.
And it will kind of simplify, if you will, like the scope, which the pods are working under.
And so I'm sure we're going to learn a lot after we do a few of these strategy sprints.
We have about eight proposals out.
So we'll try to close a few of those this week and then start the kickoff for these strategy sprints.
And start to share what we learned from it.
So cool.
Okay.
So next slide, what we're changing to be more specific.
First of all, we want to make sure we do a better job with qualification during the sales process.
And so what that means is if they're not ready, we're setting that expectation.
We might still take them on the strategy sprint, but the expectation is going to be they might not continue.
And that's okay because we're going to get so good at the strategy sprint that the amount of effort to do an additional incremental, incremental strategy.
G-Sprint is going to be, you know, with every run, should be easier, better, faster, right?

And so what we're essentially doing here with this paid scoping period is we're getting paid to figure out the scope and expectations, whereas most companies, what they would do with large deal sizes like ours, which is $100,000 to $300,000 a year, what they would do is normally go through a two to three month sales cycle where they're setting the strategy, they're doing a full audit, they're doing all this free work, if you will, right?
And so instead, what we're doing is we're getting paid to do the strategy and all the scoping that most high-end agencies would do it as part of their sales process.
And so that allows us to move a lot faster in the sales process because people are not committing to anything long-term.
They're committing to a set scope, and we're getting paid to deliver on that.
We're going to learn a ton, even if they don't continue on as a customer.
And there's multiple conversion paths.
One is into the...

The execution phase or into a platform only customer as well, or completely disqualifying them after the fact, but even if we disqualify them after the strategy sprint, they're still going to walk away pretty happy because we're going to deliver so much value, right?
And so I kind of touched a little bit on number three, but the idea is almost having packages into the editorial work stream.
So think of it as small, medium, large.
Right now, we're going to standardize with just one size.
And after the strategy sprint, we'll align on what the teams are going to be, what we're recommending to continue, but there will be a default that it goes into.
So we won't need to do a change order or a new PO.
And so that should help streamline that process.
And if there's anything above and beyond that, we would set that expectation as part of us during the strategy sprint and tell them, hey, this is what we're recommending.
Here's opportunities that we identified in this process.
These are the opportunities we
We can tackle down the line, but these are opportunities for you to keep in mind, or these are opportunities that we can start tackling right away if you choose to as well, right?
And then from there, it will go into a 12-month term, and we're going to try to push the 12-month to be paid up front as much as possible.
But then there's flexible scope, if you will, as far as if there's non-material changes, right?

And so, for instance, if in one of these cycles, when we get into the execution period, if they need more kind of graphics, hands-on, or custom workflows, we might lower our delivery on how many articles we're producing and, you know, in order to go focus on that for a cycle.
So, and then for now, we're pausing anything related to programmatic SEO that is as far as the sales process, right?
Until we get our platform ready, and for those of you that don't know, like by June 16th...
We'll all be onboarded into our own platform, that's kind of a hard date here, and we'll share more details here pretty soon.
Awesome.
Okay, cool.

I'll go through this super quickly, so we have a few minutes for questions, but if you have questions, if you want to post it in the chat, go ahead and then Bridget can watch for those and surface it up to me.
Okay, so we have two phases.
So if you hear strategy sprint, that's what it is.
It's six to eight weeks.
It's defined as eight weeks, but we've already had prospects that ask if we can deliver sooner.

Our goal is to be able to really figure out all the different jobs to be done and just get really, really, really, really, really good at all those jobs to be done within the strategy sprint and build custom workflows so that we should be able to deliver these in four weeks if necessary, right?
But then there's a lot of things around how quickly they can turn around, like the feedback and things like that.
And then...
Will default into a phase two if both don't opt out, right?

And so we can opt out, they can opt out, they'll go into a 12-month execution, but prior to even that, they can opt out at any point, and we can opt out at any point, but they still owe us for the $20,000.
We're still figuring out the exact cost, we'll most likely be $20,000 right now is what we're pitching for these deliverables in the strategy sprint.
So that's the two phases.
Phase one, we're really kind of calling this almost a AI ray artifacts that we're producing for them.
So we're helping them define ICP personas, validating some of the pain points.
A lot of what we would do is part of our onboarding process, really.
We're setting an editorial roadmap of both opportunities for net new pages, as well as existing pages on their site, and setting the next three to six months' worth of content.
And

And we're creating a lot of briefs around our competitors, and in order to gain a deeper understanding of how they're positioned in the market, we're defining really strong, like, voice and style guides that are AI-ready so they can use it.
And the idea here is, even if they decide not to use our platform, hopefully some of these artifacts are high value.
And when we say AI-ready, these are things that can be put into a prompt and use, even if they just want to go ahead and use something like ChatGPT or Clot, right?

And then we're creating some benchmark content, so one of the three assets that showcase what done looks like, and we're agreeing on our definition of done as part of this, and we're doing some technical audit on their site as well to make sure their website is healthy and any opportunities to optimize their website overall.
So these are just kind of like the basics of it.
And if we go on the next slide, we're selling this as a $20,000 flat fee.
There are some...

There some exceptions here where if the scope of the scoping sprint, if you will, or the strategy sprint is really large, like right now we have a potential customer called Augment Code, they're a competitor of like Bolt and a few others, so that one is going to require a lot more engineering involvement as part of the strategy sprint, and so we might increase this flat fee to like 30,000, for example, right, and we have a dedicated sprint squad It's going to be George, myself, there will be at least one engineer involved, one managing editor, and one content manager involved, and we're finalizing that team, and making sure like whichever pod we're taking these folks from, we'll have enough coverage as well, and I already talked about the mutual alignment, but that's pretty much it, and so it's eight weeks, and the last two weeks of this will be a transition into the

And then, super quickly, phase two, we'll go into growth execution, and there's a lot more here that we'll define, and so I won't go into too many details here, but it's pretty much what we're doing today, but more structure, and then we're in execution mode, but the difference is once we go into execution mode, we have a definition of done, we have alignment on what great looks like, we have alignment on what goals we want to hit, and what expectations are there, as far as what we're going to deliver, as far as outputs, and so, yeah.

And we can move to some, yeah, there's some questions, and I think we covered everything, so I'll just leave it there, just in case, but, so Vivek was asking, will MEs have any input on the editorial roadmap and topic clusters?
Yes, but what we're trying to do here is pick, like, this strategy as much as possible, and we'll calibrate, within week four to eight.

So to make sure like we're getting input from the pods that we were hoping to hand it off to, however, like what we don't want to do is what's happening today, which is we're like context switching too much between, especially in the onboarding phase where you might be in the execution phase over here, and over here you might be in the strategy mindset, right, and so for, but if there's domain expertise, so for example, Vivek, if we have a fintech customer coming in, like obviously we're going to want to consult you and others, right, like if there's someone that based on the background of different MEs and different directors will definitely want to consult, but the goal is to, over time, it's not going to be one single team doing all these strategy sprints, right, like there might be a couple of different teams doing it, but they're specialized in the strategy sprints, which is almost like a different muscle, and the goal is to learn here, right, we're not going to get it perfect, but we want to make sure we're changing things quickly.
Because definitely there's a lot of things not working right now.
Perfect.
And then the last question, which I saw twice, anything we're changing for current customers?

Current customers, I would say we're going to structure things a lot more and get, make sure all the stuff that's happening in the strategy sprint, we're retroactively doing it and realigning the customers on it.

And so if there's any part of this, the deliverables as part of the strategy sprint that maybe we didn't do or didn't do it the best way possible, we're going to redo and make sure that it's super aligned.
And then other than that, it's just making sure we're more consistent on the day to day and how we're doing updates and how we're tracking performance and just standardizing things a bit more.
You know, so, but the, the P zero here, the urgent thing is changing how we take on customers using the strategy sprint is both alignment and filtering.

So there should be a lot less of like the, oh my God, I need to do an onboarding.
Wait, this customer left.
Now I need to do this thing over here, right?
Like it should bring more stability into the pods.

With all new customers, there shouldn't be new customers added to the existing pods for, you know, eight weeks at least right now.
And that combined with, we did have a few customers drop off in the last few weeks.
The ratios are actually pretty strong right now that we can like really double down on the existing customers.
There should be a lot more bandwidth to be able to like tackle these issues.
So anybody who's like noticing clients struggling, like please scream at the top of your lungs, like how, what's happening and how we can fix it.
Cause we have plenty of people on the team.
Now we can all rally around that to help.

It's like a lot more bandwidth because we're not shoving new customers at people right now.

If you have questions, please ask in Slack.
And this is a work in progress.
We're just going to learn as we go.
And we'll provide lots of feedback just once we get like market, market reactions and all of that.
So thanks everyone and enjoy your weeks.
